data.title: Tariff Mayhem: Updated Asset Class Outlook
data.description:
Overview

Stocks are suffering one of the worst selloffs since March 2020. The difference is tariffs are inflationary, while the pandemic and global economic shutdown were (initially) deflationary. This means a different Federal Reserve reaction function, a reluctance to lower rates at the pace the market desires according to CME Fed Fund futures, and an inability to launch quantitative easing or QE thereby alleviating global USD shortages. Tariffs reduce the chance of Fed rate cuts and give reason to prolong the quantitative tightening program due to inflation becoming persistent in the US economy and expectations for future inflation potentially moving higher as corporate cost pressures intensify, margins are squeezed, and price increases are passed on to the consumer. This combination of tariffs and tighter Fed policy push up US yields and interest rates as inflation expectations (driven higher by tariffs) are a component of UST yields as well as the monetary policy factor.

Trade tensions are expected to slow global growth significantly. The ECB and Bank of Japan as well as many emerging market central banks including the PBOC will likely lower rates while the Fed cannot. The US dollar should be propelled higher due to these divergences. While there has been dollar weakness, it is mostly euro-related, which has a disproportionately high weighting on the DXY index. This article will cover below why euro strength cannot be sustained. On a trade-weighted basis which factors in emerging market currencies, the USD remains very resilient at a minimum, if not considered strong at the early stages of this trade war. China has been hit with over 100% duties on exports into the US and also must ease monetary policy. The USD/CNY exchange rate is at the top end of its historical range and 9.00 on the USD/CNY should not be considered off the table near-term, indicating much further weakness in the yuan and broad-based dollar strength. Currency wars and a race to the bottom (barring the USD) may ensue in global FX markets as central banks in Europe, Japan, and emerging markets try to maintain the competitiveness of their exports and overcome the tariffs while insulating their economies from growth headwinds.

US and Global Economic Outlook

The most important point regarding the trade war is the US runs a trade deficit, so the upward effect on the CPI will be greater in the United States while the negative effect on growth due to lower net exports will be greater for the rest-of-the-world growth and economies that rely on a trade surplus such as Germany and China. Even with the dialing back and 90-day pause of the initial "reciprocal tariffs", the newer tariff mix given the 100% plus rate on Chinese imports will actually have an equal to or greater effect on the CPI and prices of consumer goods than the original plan. The US economy is still facing the highest tariffs since the 1930s. This is shown below.

The President of the NY Fed, John Williams, expects the Fed's targeted inflation measure known as core PCE to accelerate to 3.5% to 4% in the US. With the recent decline in oil prices and downgraded forecasts for the global economy and world GDP growth (which correlates with oil demand), it can be expected for the headline CPI to fall despite the upwards effect of tariffs on the core rate, which excludes food and energy prices. The combination of a rising core CPI which binds the Fed's ability to ease policy while weak global growth, lower oil prices, and a hard landing for the Chinese economy drive the headline rate lower is a deadly combination for risk assets such as stocks that have been relying on the expectation of a lower Fed Funds rate, strong global economic growth and a positive global business outlook across developed and emerging market economies.

China is experiencing secular growth headwinds and deflationary forces due to the stress in the real estate, construction, and investment demand-driven growth model for the Chinese economy, which is experiencing diminishing returns. The Chinese economic authorities are relying on consumer rebalancing to occur though this cannot be expected to happen overnight and also not without stress on the highly leveraged Chinese banking system as most of the loans are lent to the "old" industrial economy and real estate development companies. Chinese net exports were being counted on to pick up the slack in the interim, yet the tariffs threw a giant wrench into that plan.

In short, China must ease policy with lower rates and devalue the yuan to stay competitive in global export markets, essentially allowing China to export its deflation onto the world and uphold its growth while awaiting consumer rebalancing. The problem is excessive yuan weakness, or a disorderly devaluation has shown to be a risk-off phenomenon in the past as there is a significant amount of corporate debt in China, particularly real estate companies, that is denominated in US dollars. Therefore, a strong USD/CNH rate increases the real burden of Chinese corporate debt, weighing on credit ratings, debt service capacity, and banking system capital stress.

I continue to expect no US recession. US GDP growth and aggregate demand will likely surprise near term as buyers front-load purchases of goods to avoid tariff-induced price increases. The labor market remains resilient, and Fed rate cuts at the end of last year are still working their way through the economy due to long and variable lags of monetary policy.

Rates and Bonds

Last week the most rapid selloff in 10Y US Treasuries since 2001 and 30Y US bonds since 1982 happened which the Fed largely turned a blind eye to. I currently have a 5.00% near-term target yield on the 10-year US Treasury bond with risk to the upside.

The Fed has expressed contentment with the current stance of policy with concerns a bit hawkish regarding tariff-induced inflation becoming persistent and ingrained into the economy. Federal Reserve president of Kansas City, Susan Collins, said:

I see monetary policy as well positioned to address a wide range of potential economic outcomes in this highly uncertain environment... Maintaining the current monetary policy stance seems appropriate for the time being.

New York Fed President, John Williams, said the current policy stance of moderately restrictive "remains appropriate".

Meanwhile, European Central Bank officials are sounding the alarm. ECB chair, Christine Lagarde said the central bank is ready to use instruments it has if needed which is a very dovish message alerting the markets to likely lower ECB deposit rates ahead and potentially asset (European bond) purchase programs pending how quickly and negatively the economic deterioration and deflationary pressures intensify in Europe. This would lead to lower yields across the common currency union. The widely watched ZEW Germany Economic Expectations Index plunged from around fifty to negative fourteen. It is linked to German GDP growth below.

The previous week was the worst ever for US bond prices versus German bunds signaling widening yield premiums of UST bonds over German yields. This should in theory push up the USD versus the dollar against the euro despite the initial knee-jerk by the market out of all US-based assets.

There are still a lot of unanswered questions and unaddressed issues regarding the explosive US government deficit and bond issuance, thereby increasing the supply of US Treasuries in the banking system. With ongoing quantitative tightening from the Fed (negative demand) and the unlikeliness of this to be reversed to QE, there is a supply-demand imbalance for US bonds.

Another factor in the UST market is foreign demand. International selling of treasuries to raise dollars can come into play when forecasting the outlook for US government bond yields. For one, selling US debt securities could be a retaliatory technique used by China in the trade war. Secondly, there is a large figure of USD-denominated emerging market corporate debt and also potentially a need by EM central banks to intervene in foreign exchange markets in order to defend their currencies from excessive and rapid depreciation as well as capital flight. This effort to raise US dollars is done through selling the international counter asset, the US Treasury bond. Bond prices and yields move inversely, so as US bonds are sold by foreign entities in an international rush to dollars, it also pushes up the US Treasury yield further incentivizing dollar purchasing through developed market carry trades into the USD or out of emerging market currencies that used the dollar as a funding currency.

FX

As stated, a few paragraphs above, yield differentials should be expected to drive foreign exchange rates over the medium term rather than emotional reactionary trading to tariffs and immediate flight from all dollar assets. There has rarely been a divergence between US nominal 10Y yields and the DXY dollar index over the last two years.

On a slightly different note, in defense of global macroeconomic investing and a return to the golden age of 1980s and 1990s discretionary macro trading, markets are in a state of flux currently and there is a high degree of uncertainty, which is a perfect environment to generate alpha. In short, the first movers will lead and when the dust settles, we see which positions are correct. The lack of consensus and the general wide range of outcomes in the global economy and financial markets is a great environment for macro trading right now.

Also, as mentioned previously, the dollar is currently exhibiting weakness versus the euro currently, though it is not broad-based against a wider range of trading partners. The strength in the euro cannot be expected to continue, as yield differentials between US and European bonds are widening rapidly. This creates a carry opportunity for FX traders to earn around 180bp borrowing euros, converting to dollars, and investing in higher-yielding US bonds, also standing to gain is the asset currency, or USD in this case, appreciates versus the funding currency or the euro. This is also a popular trade using the yen as a funding currency as Japanese government yields remain below UST yields.

There is also a common theory in FX markets known as the USD smile (shown below). The USD smile theory states the USD outperforms in strong US-centric growth environments as well as benefits from safe-haven flows in weak global economic environments. We may be seeing both, where the US economy and inflation relatively accelerate compared to the rest of the world. The euro is traditionally a very risk-on procyclical strong global growth currency which also goes along with the USD smile theory.

Equities

I am widely bearish on equities across the board currently, but after an initial risk-off sweep likely to happen, there will be sectors in US indexes that outperform as well as pockets of global equities that can thrive in the new environment.

Japan and European equities remain attractive compared to the US as they trade at a lower valuation and stand to benefit from easier Bank of Japan and ECB policies while the Fed stands pat.

The outlook for emerging market stocks remains bearish as EM equities will likely be the epicenter of the unfolding global growth slowdown, as well as foreign exchange market concerns as EM central banks try to cushion growth without creating speculative runs by large FX traders on their currencies.

In the US, the idea is to be very defensive and sector-selective. Some examples of industries with positive outlooks include banks, airlines, biotech, and oil tankers. US banks should benefit from the steepening of the US yield curve and lack of domestic recession, as well as insulation from the fragile Chinese financial system. Airlines may see their costs decrease and profits rise as oil prices come down, reducing jet fuel expenses. Biotech remains a defensive and innovative sector that trades below the P/E multiple of the S&P and Tech sector. Lastly, oil tankers excel in lower oil price environments as an oversupply of oil and increased pumping by OPEC or even price wars lead to WTI and Brent inventory builds, restocking by traders at low prices, contango storage trades, and increased transportation of oil. Trump's maximum pressure campaign and a crackdown on the "shadow" tanker fleet exporting sanctioned Russian and Iranian crude is also positive for the compliant and legal tanker operators.

Energy

The most recent OPEC meeting was a seismic shift in policy by increasing production far beyond what the market expected. It was exactly the opposite policy of what has been implemented since 2020. OPEC, specifically Saudi Arabia, the de-facto leader, seemingly abandoned the adamant clinging to keeping the futures curve in backwardation. OPEC had been keeping the futures curve in backwardation (downward sloping) by limiting production. This kept front-dated futures prices high relative to the back end of the futures curve and led to a negative carry-on holding an inventory of oil. This led to a persistent semblance of a shortage as traders and those storing oil were penalized through negative carry in holding oil, which further kept the front-end futures prices pinned higher than the back end.

While effective in keeping prices elevated higher than what they otherwise would have been, the cost came in lost market share for OPEC as US shale and rest of world production ramped up at high prices. OPEC has the lowest operating costs in the world and can win any price war versus an unhedged US shale producer, for example.

This competition for market share led to a surge in OPEC members such as Iraq and the UAE, effectively overproducing to the point where Saudi Arabia has lost patience. It seems OPEC has surrendered to the curve inevitably falling into contango (upward sloping) due to weaker global growth, leading to lower spot prices and a return to OPEC prioritizing market share over selling prices.

Gold

Lastly, gold remains the most overcrowded and overvalued asset class in the world. The inverse correlation between inflation-indexed UST yields and gold prices has broken down temporarily but can be expected to hold over the medium term for patient and opportunistic traders willing to short gold. With the USD likely to move higher due to its safe haven role as the go-to risk-off currency as described above and the unsustainability of euro strength, gold appears vulnerable.

data.title: ASML: Time To Buy Is Now, Or Regret Later
data.description:
ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) reported its first quarter earnings on April 16. In response to the report, the stock fell. In this article, I will discuss the results and reasons for the uncertainty, and I will explain why I believe ASML remains a strong buy.

ASML Earnings Nearly Double

First quarter sales were €7.7 billion, which was within the €7.5-€8 billion range that the company had guided for and beat analyst estimates. Also. 26% of the sales came from installed based management sales, which are the aftermarket sales.

Gross margins of 54% came in above expectations, driven by the achievement of productivity milestones and higher average sales prices due to the EUV and configuration mix. On sales growth of 46%, ASML achieved 97% higher operating income as R&D expenses only grew 12.5% and SG&A grew 2.7%, providing a favorable pass-through of gross profits to the operating profit level.

Net bookings stood at €3.9 billion, of which 31% was driven by EUV machines. Bookings missed estimates of €4.8 billion, and that has been one of the reasons why the stock fell following the earnings release. A healthy order inflow is, of course, needed to comfortably run and ramp the business. However, a recurring element with ASML has become that the stock is being punished based on bookings, leaving the stellar financial performance underappreciated.

The sales breakdown showed that EUV machines accounted for 56% of sales, up from 46% in the prior year quarter, driven by higher EUV shipments. By region, we saw that China represented 27% compared to 49% in the same quarter last year. For 2025, the share of sales to China is expected to be around 20%. So, what we're seeing is that sales share to China is lower, but the sales share is normalizing to historical levels a bit slower than anticipated.

ASML Maintains Guidance For 2025

For 2025, ASML maintains its guidance for €30 billion to €35 billion in sales. This is consistent with our modeling of backlog in our report on ASML.

The guidance implies growth of 6% to 24% after the company realized just 2.6% growth in sales in 2024. Margins are set to be between 51% to 53%, with the midpoint indicating a 70-basis points expansion compared to 2024.

For Q2 2025, the company expects €7.2 billion to €7.7 billion in sales, indicating 19% growth year-on-year. For 2025, China is expected to represent 20% of the sales, which is consistent with the country’s share in sales before 2023.

It's a strong sign that ASML is maintaining its guidance for 2025, but it's evident that there are growing risks and concerns.

China Continues To Be A Major Risk To ASML

ASML definitely has a lot of positives. The company is shipping a higher productivity version of the NXE:3800E, and it's ahead in system maturity on its high numerical aperture systems or High-NA EUV. It shows that EUV is a nearly constant work in process just like the other lithography systems, and since ASML is a market leader it can further strengthen its dominance and increase sales prices and margins due to iterative improvements driving higher productivity.

The current trade turmoil is providing a significant risk to chip demand. Most tariffs have been delayed for 90 days, but the tariffs against China are not part of that and have been increased. So, the sales risk to China is increasing. China’s sales primarily seem to be services and KrF and I-line sales, as the country was already banned from EUV and ArFi sales. Direct sales to China could be further impacted, either due to the tariffs that dry up demand or export controls.

Furthermore, there's an indirect impact in case more chips are added to export controls, which could somewhat reduce demand for the lithography systems used to produce those chips.

Furthermore, the tariffs could have an impact on the global economy and impact global chip demand. Consumer goods and electronics seem to be have been temporarily excluded from the higher US tariffs, so that does provide some shielding, but there's just a lot of uncertainty at this point.

A positive and partial offset could come from Europe as Europe has to establish a stronger manufacturing capability of advanced chips, but that will not happen overnight.

ASML Remains A Strong Buy With Double-Digit Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

In my prior assessment, I had a $873 price target for 2025. That target is now increasing to $943 as I expect that ASML will use the lower stock prices to execute its share buyback program. As a result, I'm lifting the price target to $948, implying 48% upside. This is supported by 17% annual growth on EBITDA and includes EBITDA pressure in 2026 as well as 6% annual growth in free cash flow with 15% pressure in 2025 and 8% in 2026.

So, there's some pressure on the estimates, but if we look at the reported results, balance sheet and cash flow, I believe this remains a strong buy, especially since ASML remains the top manufacturer of semiconductor equipment. There may be macroeconomic weakness, which may weaken the stock price, but I do believe that long-term demand remains robust and lower share prices offer a strong buy opportunity.

Conclusion: ASML Remains A Compelling Investment

There's little doubt that ASML faces uncertainties in the current macroeconomic environment. However, I also do believe that the strong results and positioning in the market are currently not being appreciated nearly enough as investors and analysts are looking at bookings.

Bookings are definitely important as they provide some forward-looking insights. However, while chip demand growth may flatten in the current macroeconomic landscape, I do expect it to snap back eventually and chip manufacturers simply need ASML’s state-of-the-art lithography systems if they want to serve chip demand with the most advanced chips. So, there's no way around ASML in that regard.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AltaGas: An Interesting Combination Of Midstream And Utility
data.description:
AltaGas Ltd. (OTCPK:ATGFF) is an energy infrastructure company based in Canada that operates in two segments. These two segments are midstream energy and utilities:

Both midstream energy and utilities are sectors that I have written extensively about for good reason. In short, companies that operate in these two sectors tend to enjoy remarkably stable cash flows all through the business cycle, and they tend to possess fairly high dividend yields. AltaGas is no exception to this, as its 3.18% current yield is significantly higher than that of either the S&P 500 Index (SP500) (SPY) or the MSCI Canada Custom Capped Index (EWC):

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index

1.36%

MSCI Canada Custom Capped Index

2.20%

The current yield of AltaGas is also higher than the 2.32% of the U.S. Utilities Index (IDU). However, it is worth noting that AltaGas has a significantly lower yield than that of its Canadian midstream peers:

Company

Current Yield

Enbridge Inc. (ENB)

5.96%

Pembina Pipeline Corporation (PBA)

5.29%

TC Energy Corporation (TRP)

5.01%

As such, investors whose goal is maximizing their income may be somewhat disappointed here. It is also worth noting that AltaGas has substantially trailed all of the above companies on a total return basis over the past ten years:

As such, a cursory look at the company reveals that there may not be a lot to attract a potential investor. However, we should keep in mind that past performance is no guarantee of future results. It is certainly possible that the company’s stock performance will improve going forward. Indeed, we can already see that starting to occur as AltaGas has outperformed all of its Canadian midstream peers over the past three years:

The above chart shows the total return of all of these assets and not just the share price performance. As such, we can clearly see that the price performance of AltaGas was substantially better than that of its peers over the period, since it was sufficient to overcome the boost that the peer stocks got by virtue of their higher yields and still come out on top. This could suggest that the company might be worth considering for a potential investment, and that we should take a closer look at it.

About AltaGas

As stated in the introduction, AltaGas is a Canadian energy infrastructure company that operates in both the midstream energy segment and the utilities segment. However, it is frequently considered to be a utility company by financial websites and many analysts. For example, Seeking Alpha considers it to be a natural gas utility for comparison purposes and provides the following peer group for the company:

We can see that the site has placed it in with other natural gas utilities. This could make some sense, as the company’s most recent guidance states that it expects 55% of its normalized EBITDA to come from the utility segment, with only 45% coming from midstream energy. In addition, many natural gas utilities operate a midstream segment that transports natural gas. For example, DT Midstream (DTM) was once part of DTE Energy (DTE), and Atmos Energy (ATO) owns Atmos Pipeline-Texas, which is a natural gas midstream company. However, for most of these companies, the natural gas pipeline network accounts for a lower proportion of their EBITDA than is the case for AltaGas.

Another thing that differentiates AltaGas from some of its natural gas utility peers is that the midstream entity serves a vastly different area of the world than its utility operation. In this case, the midstream company is not intended to supplement its natural gas distribution operations but is intended to be a standalone midstream company in its own right. We can see this in the fact that the company’s pipelines and natural gas storage facilities are primarily located in western Canada:

We do see that the company owns a railroad that runs along the northern border of the United States from the Bakken Shale to Ferndale, Washington, but overall, the majority of its midstream infrastructure is in Canada, serving the Western Canada Sedimentary Basin. In this respect, it is not unlike Pembina Pipeline, which serves a similar area.

AltaGas’s utility operations, on the other hand, serve a vastly different area. The company owns two regional utility companies – Washington Gas and Semco Energy. Washington Gas serves the area around the District of Columbia:

As such, we can expect that a large number of its customers either work for the Federal government or in private sector roles that support government operations. This could be important given some of the changing demographics that appear to be occurring in the area around Washington, D.C. In particular, the media has been delivering rather opinionated coverage of the layoffs that have been occurring across the Federal government over the past few months. Regardless of your opinion of these layoffs, they have had an effect that investors in AltaGas should not ignore. This is that many people appear to be relocating out of the District of Columbia and the surrounding area. As Zero Hedge points out, the current MLS listings for residential properties in this area are 47.5% higher than at this same time last year:

We immediately note that several of the counties shown above are identical to the ones served by AltaGas’s subsidiary Washington Gas. This could suggest that the company’s customer base is likely to decline in the near term, which would, of course, have an adverse impact on the revenues that AltaGas generates from this subsidiary. This is particularly the case if the houses that have been going up for sale struggle to sell, which may end up being the case unless Washington, D.C., sees a boom in private sector hiring. That seems unlikely in the near term, given the area’s dependence on the Federal government (and Federal government spending), which is decreasing. As such, this may represent the early signs of a headwind for this aspect of AltaGas’s business.

The second utility business owned by AltaGas is Semco Energy Gas Company, which serves various areas around the state of Michigan:

The areas in blue on the map above represent the areas in which Semco Gas has distribution pipelines and provides natural gas utility service to homes and businesses. This utility alone is actually fairly large, especially when compared to some of the other utilities that we have discussed in this column in recent weeks. Semco Energy’s website states that it serves about 300,000 customers throughout the blue service territory shown above. This makes it roughly three times the size of a company such as Unitil (UTL), which serves just under 100,000 natural gas customers. This may not be surprising, though, given that Michigan is one of the larger states in the country. World Population Review ranks the state as tenth in the country, with a population slightly larger than New Jersey’s and slightly lower than that of North Carolina’s.

The state of Michigan, in general, appears to be benefiting from positive demographic growth, at least when compared to the District of Columbia. According to World Population Review, the state’s population is growing at a 0.56% rate, which would be sufficient to bring the current estimated population of 10,197,600 up to approximately 10.4 million by the end of the decade:

This is certainly an improvement from the generally stagnant to declining population that the state had in the 2000 to 2020 period. However, we should keep in mind that just because the state’s population as a whole is growing, there is no guarantee that Semco Energy’s service territory will experience positive customer growth. However, this is still a better situation than what the company appears to be experiencing in the Washington, D.C. area.

Data Center Opportunity for Washington Gas

With that said, there are certainly some opportunities for the company to grow its revenue in the Washington, D.C. area that does not require a growing customer base. One of the most significant of these opportunities comes in the form of data centers, which represent a fairly large opportunity for many utilities to generate growth over the coming few years. I discussed this opportunity in a previous article.

Ashburn, Virginia, is one of the largest technology hubs in the world. In fact, some industry analysts believe that approximately 70% of all global internet traffic goes through a data center in Ashburn at some point in its journey between the requesting user and the server where the information can be found. This one city contains 29% of all data centers currently in existence in the United States:

This city is also expected to be one of the areas where a large number of data centers are constructed going forward. This actually makes a lot of sense given that the area is already home to a large population of professionals who have experience with managing and running data centers, as well as an extensive amount of telecommunications infrastructure to handle the traffic coming into and out of these data centers. For this reason, PJM Interconnection, which is a regional transmission organization that coordinates the movement of electricity through thirteen states and the District of Columbia, projects that the power demand from data centers constructed in this area over the next ten years will increase at a 15% compound annual growth rate:

As we can see here, Ashburn, Virginia, is firmly within the area covered by PJM Interconnection’s purview:

As such, we can expect their projections to be about as accurate as we can get based on the current plans of the technology and data center industries. If the area’s power consumption increases at a 15% compound annual growth rate just due to data centers alone, we can expect that the actual increase in power consumption will be higher than this due to things such as employees of the new data centers building homes in the area, not to mention electricity consumption from businesses that are set up to provide goods and services to the influx of new technology employees.

This creates two opportunities for Washington Gas, and by extension, Altagas. They come from the fact that Ashburn, Virginia, is within the company’s natural gas service territory. As technology employees construct homes within this area to be close to their jobs, they will need to acquire utility services to provide heat within their homes. Natural gas is usually considered to be the fuel of choice for this purpose due to being cheaper than propane, heating oil, or electricity. As such, the people who construct new homes in Ashburn or in the neighboring communities will likely opt to purchase natural gas from the company if possible. This will provide a revenue increase from the new customers paying their monthly bills.

A second, and perhaps more significant, source of growth for Washington Gas comes from the data centers themselves. There has been a trend among data center designers and engineers recently to add onsite power generation to meet their energy needs. This is mostly because the public utility grid is not always robust enough to handle the power draw of a large data center without potentially causing problems for other customers, as well as significant delays from utilities that sometimes require five to seven years before connecting a data center to the electric grid. The usual way that the onsite power generation is obtained is by adding natural gas turbines to the data center itself. Basically, a small natural gas power plant is constructed that provides electricity for the data center. Naturally, this would require someone to provide the natural gas needed to run this turbine, and Washington Gas could be a reasonable choice for any data centers constructed in the Ashburn, Virginia area.

AltaGas has already identified this as a potential opportunity for the future and claims to be positioning itself to satisfy this emerging need within the area. It remains to be seen how much growth this will actually provide, but it could be one of the better opportunities for the company’s utility business in the near term.

Midstream Opportunities

AltaGas’s midstream operation is slightly smaller than its utility arm, as mentioned earlier. The company states that about 45% of its normalized EBITDA comes from the midstream unit:

Thus, this business unit is slightly less important for the company’s overall financial performance than is its utility arm. However, given that it still accounts for 45% of the company’s normalized EBITDA, we should not ignore it.

There is a great opportunity for this segment to generate some growth due to the growing international demand for liquefied petroleum gas, specifically propane and butane. According to Wood Mackenzie, Asian demand for liquefied petroleum gas will grow over the 2015 to 2050 period:

We can, however, see that the rate of growth begins to decline after 2030. Thus, the greatest growth potential from this trend comes over the next five years. However, the demand certainly does not go away and should continue to grow going forward. Overall, we see a 42% increase in Asian propane imports and a 48% increase in Asian butane imports over the 2022 to 2040 period:

Back in 2020, AltaGas assumed operatorship of the Ferndale LPG Terminal, which handles propane and butane. This facility is located in Ferndale, Washington, where it has direct access to the Pacific Ocean. In fact, the facility has an on-site dock that is capable of handling Very Large Gas Carriers, which are the largest liquefied petroleum gas tankers in operation today. The largest of these ships are capable of carrying in excess of 90,000 cubic meters of propane or butane. Thus, the Ferndale LPG Terminal is well-positioned to serve as a hub for liquefied petroleum gas exports to Asian markets, which are growing their demand for these substances.

Financial Considerations

AltaGas has a reasonable financial structure for either a utility company or a midstream company. As of December 31, 2024, the company had a net debt-to-equity ratio of 1.16. Here is how that compares to the utility peers that were provided earlier in this article:

Company

Net Debt-to-Equity

AltaGas Ltd.

1.16

National Fuel Gas Co. (NFG)

1.03

UGI Corporation (UGI)

1.53

Southwest Gas Holdings (SWX)

1.31

New Jersey Resources (NJR)

1.55

ONE Gas (OGS)

1.06

As we can see, AltaGas generally has a fairly conservative net debt-to-equity ratio when compared to its peers. The median ratio of the above companies is 1.24, so AltaGas is obviously well below that. It also has a ratio that is less than three out of the five peer companies, which clearly suggests that AltaGas is not relying too much on debt to finance its operations.

Of course, the above comparison is only valid if we are comparing the company to utilities. As AltaGas is both a utility and a midstream company, we should compare it to some of its Canadian midstream peers as well:

Company

Net Debt-to-Equity

AltaGas Ltd.

1.16

Enbridge Inc.

1.47

Pembina Pipeline Corporation

0.75

TC Energy Corporation

1.54

For the most part, AltaGas compares fairly well to this peer group as well. The company is, unfortunately, substantially more leveraged than Pembina Pipeline, but it compares fairly well with its other peers. With that said, regular readers will likely remember that I have been fairly critical about both Enbridge’s and TC Energy’s use of debt to finance themselves in the past, and both companies are much more leveraged than some of the American midstream competitors. However, we can see that AltaGas is quite reasonably financed for a Canadian company in its industry.

Based on this, we can conclude that AltaGas has a fairly reasonable financial structure that should satisfy even the most risk-averse investor. The company does not appear to be taking undue risks with respect to its usage of debt.

Valuation

AltaGas’s current share price could be undervalued compared to its peers. For example, here is how it compares to the other utilities in its peer group:

Company

TTM P/E Ratio

AltaGas Ltd.

21.20

National Fuel Gas Co.

22.86

UGI Corporation

26.31

Southwest Gas Holdings

26.54

New Jersey Resources

14.95

ONE Gas

19.69

The company is not the cheapest in its peer group, but it still compares fairly well with this peer group when valuing it using this metric. In particular, the company is in the bottom half when measured as the amount of money that an investor has to pay today for each dollar of earnings that it has earned in the past year.

Let us see how it compares to its Canadian Midstream peers:

Company

TTM P/E

AltaGas Ltd.

21.20

Enbridge Inc.

27.32

Pembina Pipeline Corporation

17.94

TC Energy Corporation

17.38

Here, it is much harder to make a case for the company at its current price. AltaGas has a price that is more expensive than two of the three peers. It is significantly cheaper than Enbridge, but to my mind, Enbridge is a bit overpriced right now.

Overall, AltaGas certainly does not look overpriced right now. It compares fairly well with the utility sector peers, and as more than half of the company’s normalized EBITDA comes from its utility operations, it is probably more appropriate to compare it to those companies than it is to the Canadian midstream firms. While the company does not look overpriced, it is difficult to make a case for it being cheap right now. There are a number of both utilities and Canadian midstream companies with more attractive multiples, and some of those (specifically Pembina Pipeline) have similar growth potential. Thus, the conclusion here appears to be that the company is fairly valued at the current level.

Conclusion

In conclusion, AltaGas is an interesting combination of a Canadian midstream company and a utility. These are both historically fairly stable businesses that should hold up reasonably well in the event that the United States enters into a recession in the near future. The company also has some growth potential given its focus on natural gas and natural gas liquids, as both of these are likely to benefit from growing demand over the next ten years or so.

The company is not overly expensive right now, but it is certainly not value-priced. Potential investors may want to keep that in mind, as the stock could become more attractive in the near future if the market pulls back.

data.title: USD/JPY Outlook: Relief Bounce In U.S. Dollar Before Yen Strength Resumes
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

This is a follow-up analysis of our prior report dated 18 March 2025.

Since our last publication, the USD/JPY has staged the expected corrective rebound of 1.3% to print an intraday high of 151.21 on 28 March, just a whisker below the 151.50 medium-term pivotal resistance highlighted in our analysis.

Thereafter, it staged a multi-week bearish impulsive down move of 6.3% to print a 7-month low of 141.64 on Wednesday, 16 April, reinforced by the uncertainties on the implementation of different types of US trade tariffs, and heightened tensions between the US and China that sparked an increased risk of a global economic growth slow down, in turn, benefiting safe haven currencies such as the Japanese yen and Swiss franc.

US-Japan trade talks may not be smooth sailing under the surface

The recent Japanese yen strength has taken a backseat since the start of today’s 17 April Asian session, where the USD/JPY rebounded by 0.6% to print an intraday high of 143,08 at the time of writing.

The US dollar’s bounce has been supported by US President Trump’s positive rhetoric after the conclusion of the first session of the US-Japan trade negotiations dialogue yesterday, 16 April. He touted that “big progress” has been made via his social media account.

In contrast, the key Japanese official participating in the trade talks, Ryosei Akazawa, the Economic Revitalisation Minister, was not as optimistic as he mentioned Japan will schedule the next consultation with the US to be held within this month, which suggests that there are still hurdles and frictions after yesterday's first round of trade negotiation.

US/Japan implied policy rate curve is supporting further yen strength

The implied interest rate differential between US and Japan policy interest rates, projected to December 2025 via short-term rate futures, has narrowed to 2.86% from 3.20% three months ago.

This continued compression in the yield spread could exert further downward pressure on USD/JPY, in line with historical correlations (see Fig 1).

Japan’s March inflation data will be released on Friday, 18 April, with core and core-core inflation expected to edge up to 3.2% y/y (from 3.0%) and 2.7% y/y (from 2.6%), respectively. A stronger-than-expected reading would support the Bank of Japan’s gradual tightening stance and could further narrow the US-Japan implied interest rate differential.

No bearish exhaustion condition in the MACD trend indicator

The spread of the daily MACD trend indicator and its signal line has continued to widen towards the downside (below the zero centreline), which supports the ongoing medium-term downtrend phase of the USD/JPY.

Watch the 147.30 key medium-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 140.30/140.00 intermediate support zone may trigger the continuation of the impulsive down move sequence to expose the next medium-term supports at 137.10/136.50 and 134.20/133.75 next.

However, a clearance above 147.30 invalidates the bearish tone for a corrective rebound towards the next medium-term resistances at 151.40 and 154.15.

data.title: LexinFintech: Still Bullish Even With Evolving Landscape
data.description:
LexinFintech Holdings (NASDAQ:LX) has experienced an incredible stock run since late-2024. However, the evolving economic landscape, more specifically around rising tensions between the U.S. and China, has caused over 30% pullback in the stock.

In a more normalized geopolitical environment, I believe this company has a strong foundation to support growth. Nevertheless, we are no longer in a normalized environment, and exogenous factors are starting to form into headwinds.

LexinFintech is a Chinese fintech that provides consumer financial services, primarily through its platform “Fenqile” which roughly translates to “happy installment” in Chinese. The company has been a clear beneficiary of the rise of buy-now-pay-later services, which has only accelerated since the COVID-fueled spending time period. In a more challenging spending environment, consumers may actually increase their usage of BNPL, as installment payments make larger purchases easier to pay.

Yes, there are still significant risks with the company given the combination of tense U.S.-China relations and lower consumer confidence. However, I believe this pullback presents a good buying opportunity for long-term focused investors.

Examining the Fundamentals

In my last article on LexinFintech, I discussed how this could be a multi-year bullish story, noting that I would view any pullbacks as an attractive entry point.

I remain bullish on the long-term trajectory for the company, however, given some of the broader macro challenges and the stock price potentially exceeding growth expectations, I am more hesitant to be ultra-bullish at current levels. Remaining bullish long-term and would use any pullbacks as an attractive entry point.

Buy-now-pay-later solutions have continued to grow in popularity in recent years, with younger generations often preferring this over traditional credit cards. U.S.-based competitors such as Affirm, Klarna, and Afterpay have gained significant traction, and LexinFintech is aiming to do the same with the Chinese consumer base.

According to a report by ResearchAndMarkets, the BNPL market in China grew at a nearly 20% CAGR from 2021-2024, demonstrated the strong tailwinds seen from COVID spending. Growth is expected to be at a more much subdued ~9% CAGR from 2025-2030.

Similar to other BNPL ecosystems, LexinFintech has established strong relationships with both the merchants and the consumers. The unique part of LexinFintech is their focus on the underbanked community, which often does not have access to traditional credit methods, such as credit cards, and thus must rely on installment loans like BNPL in order to finance larger transactions.

The company’s consumer focus is also much more tech-savvy, and the focus on this next-generation consumer may give LexinFintech the long-term competitive advantage. The company has built out an ecosystem of >3k merchant partners and >228 million users.

LexinFintech reported their Q4-2024 results a few weeks ago, which included revenue growth of 4% yoy. However, the company’s focus in recent quarters has been more on improving credit quality, thus accelerating profitability. For the quarter, adjusted net income grew an impressive 38% yoy.

The company is focusing more on driving recurring customer base, and this is evident with 85.3% of loans during Q4 coming from repeated borrowers' contribution. As with many financial technology companies, it is much easier and cheaper to expand with existing customers than it is to acquire new ones.

And acquiring new customers has been a bit of a challenge for LexinFintech. Yes, the 228 million user base is a significant number, and this did grow 9% yoy. However, the number of active users during Q4 was only 4.7 million, declining 1% yoy. It is not uncommon for BNPL ecosystems to have significantly more total users compared to its active user base. However, the fact that LexinFintech’s active user base is declining is a bit of a concern.

From a credit quality standpoint, trends have been consistent, with first payment default rate (30+ days) for new loan originations staying below 1%. Delinquency ratio for 90+ days was 3.6%, slightly lower than the 3.7% observed during Q3-2024.

As mentioned above, the macro environment is often the biggest swing factor for financial technology companies. A general slowdown in consumer spending typically correlates with lower loan originations, thus less growth.

LexinFintech appears to be taking a more conservative approach with their guidance and assumes total loan origination growth of flat to single-digit yoy during 2025. Some of management’s additional commentary is below.

Looking ahead to 2025, amid the current macro and industry environment, we will continue to adhere to prudent operating strategy, prioritizing risk management, and driving further de-risking and asset structure optimization. We are confident in achieving significant profit growth this year. We will strengthen our differentiated offerings in credit lines and pricings and refine our operating systems to meet the diverse financial needs of our customers at all levels, delivering high-quality services throughout their lifecycle.

The heightened focus on credit quality could prove to be extremely important in 2025.

Since the company reported earnings on March 18, a lot has changed with the macro. Most importantly, the U.S. and China have initiated tariffs, further escalating the already heightened geopolitical relationship. Although the ultimate outcome of tariffs and the impact on consumer spending is uncertain, several US-based banks with global reach have warned that consumer spending may slow down as a result of tariff uncertainty.

Even just one month removed from earnings, it appears the risk to growth is higher now. Management has taken the right steps to focus on credit quality and asset optimization; however, lower loan origination growth will negatively impact the business in 2025.

Valuation

As outlined above, there are some risks to the company’s 2025 guidance that was provided just one month ago. However, the stock has already pulled back over 30% from recent highs as investors have re-set their expectations around LexinFintech.

The challenging part from here is determining how much downside is left to go.

At face value, the company’s valuation at ~4x EPS appears very cheap. Even if loan originations turn negative for the year, the company’s internal cost improvements could still potentially yield adjusted net income growth. Investors are not likely banking on this scenario to happen in 2025, and given that the company only just reported, we are not likely to get another financial update from LexinFintech for another ~2 months.

For now, the recent pullback presents an opportunity to cautiously build up a position in the name. The 30% pullback has already discounted 2025 financial expectations, and with the market settling down a bit over the past week, we may have seen the worst already.

Risks

The biggest risk to be aware of is the general macro environment and rising tensions between the U.S. and China. Given LexinFintech’s focus on the Chinese consumer, a deteriorating relationship between the two countries could cause accelerated consumer spending pressure. Companies with China exposure often face sentiment headwinds when the geopolitical relationship becomes more strained.

Additionally, and without bringing politics into the conversation, there have already been a lot of changes within the current U.S. Administration. If this Administration were to restrict investments into China-based companies, this would be a material detriment to LexinFintech.

Another risk is the ongoing competition. This includes traditional financial institutions and digital-native commerce platforms. Larger banks such as Ant Financial, JD Finance, WeChat, Baidu, and Tencent all compete in the BNPL market.

Finally, small-cap stocks can often have a trading and liquidity issue. When looking at data from Yahoo Finance, the stock has traded in a very wide 52-week range of $1.57-$11.64, signaling the high volatility. On average, ~4.3 million shares trade each day, representing ~$30 million of trading volume, or ~2.6% of the company's market cap. The rule of thumb is that anything above 2% is generally considered to have good liquidity.

data.title: The Prime Contractor Of The New Energy Era: The Quanta Case
data.description:
Introduction

Quanta Services (NYSE:PWR) is the largest player in the energy and communications infrastructure market throughout North America. With more than 200 combined contractors and a team of over 58,000 people, the company is able to take on everything from the most complex design to urgent disaster recovery.

In 2024, revenue increased by 13.4% to $23.67 billion, and the total backlog—those orders that are already in progress or about to be signed—exceeded $34.5 billion. This is, in fact, the visibility of revenue for several years ahead. To understand the scale: Quanta accounts for about 15% of all external contracting costs in the US energy sector. This is three times more than the nearest competitor. Now the company is right in the epicenter of a huge investment cycle for infrastructure renovation.

Business model

Quanta Services is not just a contractor, but an entire ecosystem that builds the infrastructure of the future. The company has a full-fledged technological approach to energy, communications, and integrated infrastructure construction. Electric grids, wind farms, fiber optics, gas distribution, oil refining — all this is really being closed by Quanta Services. In 2024, 83% of Quanta's revenue came from fixed and unit-price contracts, which, on the one hand, provide predictability and on the other hand, maintain good margins. The company also has many long-term framework agreements with major customers, so the company's order flow is stable.

Previously, the company was mainly engaged in energy, but now it has data centers, telecom, and industrial facilities in its arsenal. In 2024, they even changed the reporting structure: they combined electrics and renewable energy into one segment—Electric Infrastructure Solutions. The second segment, Underground Utility & Infrastructure—is about gas, oil, and the entire industrial infrastructure. This business architecture allows you to close the entire cycle—from design to final completion of the facility—without involving third-party contractors.

From the point of view of capital, their model is really effective. In 2024, CapEx accounted for about 2.5% of revenue, which is a very low level for such a large—scale business. Quanta does not burn money on a fleet of vehicles, it works on a point-by-point basis: some of the equipment is rented and some is purchased as needed. The main expenses are people, materials, and subcontracting. This structure makes the business as adaptive as possible. If the market slows down, they quickly reduce costs. If, on the contrary, there is a surge in demand, they scale up calmly.

Competitors

There are many players in the infrastructure services market in North America, and there are plenty of directions, but Quanta Services has almost no direct competitors at its level. The closest in scale and profile is MasTec (MTZ). They are also building power grids, telecom, and pipelines, but at the same time, they focus on traditional energy. Quanta, especially after the Blattner takeover, has gone deep into renewable energy and electricity in general, while MasTec continues to focus on oil, gas, and main pipes. Yes, they overlap in terms of clients — Duke Energy (DUK) and Exelon (EXC) — but they often work in different parts of the same project.

There is also MYR Group (MYRG), which does well in city tenders and can compete on prices, especially in small projects. However, when it comes to large—scale stories like interstate transmission lines or integrated power systems, Quanta is out of competition in terms of scale and resources. There is also competition in the telecom segment, such as Dycom (DY), especially in fiber and 5G. But the niche itself is so overheated by demand, especially in rural areas, that competition there is more of a formality: there are enough orders for everyone. Quanta is simply expanding its product line here and working under the same contracts as in the energy sector.

As for oil and gas, Quanta used to enter main pipelines, but in recent years it has made a U-turn towards distribution networks. There is less competition, the volumes are stable, and the load is predictable. They feel confident in this niche, while players like Primoris (PRIM) or Fluor (FLR) focus more on "big" projects like refineries or LNG terminals.

In renewable energy, after the purchase of Blattner, Quanta simply became an absolute leader. The main competitors are Mortenson, Acciona (OTCPK:ACXIF), and Orsted, but they often do not have the same comprehensive competence.: They build wind farms, but they don't build power lines or substations. And Quanta can close the entire facility from the foundation to the connection to the network. And it's really valuable.

Politics in 2025 has added difficulties: the Trump administration has turned rather sharply towards traditional energy. The issuance of "green" subsidies and licenses for offshore wind turbines has been slowed down. If the renewable energy market freezes, it will affect the company's growth, especially in terms of new projects. At the same time, regulators such as FERC have simplified the approval process for fossil fuel pipelines and infrastructure under new leadership. These developments create more favorable conditions for companies involved in oil and gas construction. Major natural gas transportation projects, the construction of export LNG terminals, and the modernization of refineries can be expected to revive. If such an infrastructure boom in the traditional energy sector begins, MasTec and similar players (Primoris, engineering firms) will be in a winning position, as they have a profile close to the needs of oil and gas companies, Quanta will be more difficult.

But, on the other hand, the states continue to push their decarbonization programs, the renewable energy economy is already self-sufficient, and large customers are still planning to switch to clean energy. In addition, everyone needs an electric infrastructure — both those who install wind turbines and those who build data centers. If gas production starts to increase, it will also mean more substations, more lines, and more work.

Financials

Revenue increased from $11.2 billion in 2020 to $23.67 billion in 2024, more than doubling in four years. This trend was driven by growth in the electricity and renewable energy segments, as well as the integration of new assets, despite a moderate decline in the oil and gas sector. Today, almost half of the company's revenue is generated by electric grids, a third by renewable energy, and about 20% by underground infrastructure. At the same time, the current portfolio of orders — $34.5 billion, including $17.3 billion of short—term contracts—provides excellent visibility of future revenue.

For 2025, I expect revenue of $26.47 billion, which corresponds to an increase of almost 12%. This growth is supported by the investment cycle in infrastructure — from new transmission lines and charging stations to the replacement of old gas pipelines — and Quanta's high share of industry costs. My forecast is generally consistent with the consensus forecast, which expects revenue in 2025 in the range of $26.70-27.01 billion.

The Quanta Services business, like most contractors, traditionally operates with low margins, but this does not prevent the company from showing stable profit growth. In 2024, the gross margin reached 14.8% and the operating margin reached 5.5%, which is slightly higher than previous levels due to increased scale, high—quality contract execution, and a profitable project structure. In 2025, I expect EBITDA of about $2.21 billion with a margin of 8.3%, taking into account the integration of CEI and wage pressures. At the same time, net profit, according to my forecast, will grow to $952 million in 2025 and exceed $1.15 billion by 2027.

At the end of 2024, the company's net debt was $3.42 billion, with a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 1.83x. Most of the debt is long—term fixed-rate bonds, which minimizes the risks from rising interest rates. Quanta also has an almost unused credit facility worth $2.5 billion, which provides high liquidity. Management explicitly states the priority of deleveraging, and in 2024 the company reduced its debt by $300 million after the purchase of CEI.

The Quanta Services business, although associated with large—scale infrastructure construction, remains relatively low-capital-intensive—the main investments go into special equipment, a fleet, and tools for crews. In 2024, capital investments amounted to $604 million (2.5% of total revenue). Some of the funds were spent on upgrading equipment after stressful years of disaster recovery. I expect that in 2025 it will be at a similar level with investments of about $658 million. Part of the cost is offset by the sale of old equipment: in 2024, Quanta returned about $78 million through the sale of equipment. This allows us to keep net CapEx closer to 2% of revenue, which distinguishes Quanta from capital-intensive industries.

In 2024, the company recorded a record FCF of $1.68 billion, which was made possible by an increase in operating profit and effective working capital management, in particular, a reduction in accounts receivable and improved settlement conditions for large contracts. I expect FCF to adjust slightly down to $1.31 billion in 2025 as the normal level of current assets is restored, but it will start growing again in 2026, exceeding $2 billion by 2028. The main source of cash flow is operating profit, not one—time transactions, which indicates its sustainability.

In fiscal year 2024, the company paid $40 million in dividends, or $0.27 per share, with a yield of about 0.14%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $60 million for dividends or $0.41 per share with a yield of 0.15%.

Valuation

Now let's move on to the most painful part of my analysis – the assessment. The company's shares have grown by 9.70% over the past year due to good financial results, but at the same time, a price correction has begun since the beginning of the year. But even with the correction, the company's share price looks highly overvalued.

Firstly, the company trades at a significant premium to its historical averages and medians in terms of multipliers: the premium for P/E is 14%, for P/S – 19%, and for EV/EBITDA – 26%

Compared to peers, the company also trades at premiums in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S, and EV/EBITDA multipliers, as shown in the table below. At the same time, for example, in terms of profitability, the company is inferior to the average values – most peers are more profitable. The only advantage is the high rate of revenue growth over the past 5 years – here the company is second only to Comfort Systems (FIX).

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 40%), the target value of P/E, P/S, and EV/EBITDA with estimated weights of 20%. In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 8.34%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. The equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. The current 10-year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 39.78x, P/S is 1.43x, and EV/EBITDA is 18.67x (I took the averages for the last five years).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $210.93, which is 22.75% lower than the current price. Indeed, I believe that despite the high-quality business and high-growth rates, the company's shares are highly overvalued and at this price, I am not ready to recommend it to those investors who are currently eyeing Quanta. But I also cannot recommend that Quanta securities holders sell them, as this is a really high–quality company that is worth investing in—but at a reasonable price. Therefore, I assign the company a "Hold" rating.

Key risks

The main risk is dependence on large infrastructure investments, especially in the energy and renewable energy sectors. With the advent of Trump, federal support for "green" projects is beginning to be curtailed, which means that some of those orders that were hanging in the backlog may either be delayed or not reach the execution phase at all. At the same time, most of Quanta's revenue is based on fixed or unit contracts, where revaluation of estimates or postponement of deadlines is a blow to margins.

And given how fast salaries in construction are growing now and how badly there are not enough qualified people, the cost can eat up more profits than planned. In segments such as telecom or distribution networks, the competition is very fierce, and the winner is not always the one who is better, but often the one who is cheaper. If we add a possible recession to this, where utilities and energy companies will start cutting budgets, the growth rate of Quanta can easily slow down. If this happens against the background of large investment projects or fresh M&A, then FCF compression may temporarily worsen the financial situation.

Key growth drivers

The main driver is the global trend towards electrification of everything. Electricity consumption is growing, the number of data centers is increasing, electric vehicles require charging, renewable energy projects continue to connect to the grid—and all this requires not just modernization, but a large-scale restructuring of the entire energy infrastructure. Quanta is able to build everything from wind farms to substations and power transmission lines, on a turnkey basis, without involving a dozen contractors. At the same time, the company has a good financial position—FCF is steadily growing, the debt burden is under control, and this creates a foundation for both organic growth and new acquisition deals. The experience with CEI and Blattner has already shown that the company can scale without loss of quality. Long-term contracts, high repeatability of orders, and strong positions in key niches ensure strong revenue visibility for years to come.

Conclusion

Quanta Services enters 2025 in excellent shape. The company shows steady revenue growth, high order workload, and improved profitability. Cash flows are at a historic peak and more than cover the needs of the business. The balance sheet remains strong – although the debt has grown due to acquisitions, it is quite controllable, and its reduction has already begun. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $210.93, which is 22.75% lower than the current price. Indeed, I believe that despite the high-quality business and high-growth rates, the company's shares are highly overvalued and at this price, I am not ready to recommend it to those investors who are currently eyeing Quanta. But I also cannot recommend that Quanta securities holders sell them, as this is a really high–quality company that is worth investing in—but at a reasonable price. Therefore, I assign the company a "Hold" rating.

data.title: Emerging Markets Debt: 4 Questions For Investors
data.description:
The emerging markets (EM) debt investable universe is large, diverse, and often misunderstood. While it offers the potential for higher returns, it also comes with higher risks. To unlock alpha opportunities, it’s important to have a full understanding of this unique asset class.

What is EM Debt?

The EM debt investable universe is composed of fixed-income securities issued by EM governments and companies. Overall, EM debt consists of 900 issuers from more than 90 countries.

Securities can be issued in hard currencies or local currencies, and there are four universes within EM debt: hard currency sovereign debt, hard currency frontier markets debt, hard currency corporate debt, and local currency sovereign debt.

Hard currency debt is denominated in developed-market currencies (such as the U.S. dollar), issued in international markets, and subject to the issuing country’s laws. On the other hand, local currency debt is denominated in EM currencies (such as the Brazilian real or the Mexican peso) and predominantly issued in local markets under the issuing country’s laws.

In addition, sovereign debt is issued by a national government to finance its activities or manage its debt and is tied to a country’s fiscal health and political stability. Corporate debt is issued by corporations to raise capital for business operations and depends on the financial strength and profitability of the issuing company. One can also further narrow both of these categories down by focusing on frontier markets debt, which is issued by governments or corporations in frontier markets; these are economies that are less developed than emerging markets but have high growth potential.

EM debt is often tracked and analyzed through various indices, which serve as benchmarks for both hard currency and local currency debt. One of the most prominent indices for hard currency EM debt is the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified. For local currency debt, the J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified is widely used.

These indices are crucial for investors as they provide a framework for measuring performance, assessing risk, and making informed allocation decisions within the EM debt universe. And inclusion in these indices often signals a certain level of credibility and market access for the issuing countries, which can influence investment flows and borrowing costs.

Why Invest in EM Debt?

EM debt is a sizable asset class that has substantially grown over the years. It now represents close to a quarter of the global fixed-income universe, and is diversified across countries, currencies, and corporate credit issuers.

From a credit perspective, we believe EM debt appears solid, with strong fundamentals in both sovereign and corporate credit when compared to similar issuers in developed markets. Multilateral and bilateral institutions, such as the International Monetary Fund (IMF), provide strong support via accessible and affordable financing for EM countries as well. This helps contribute to lower default rates, as countries can secure stable funding even in unfavorable market conditions.

EM debt now represents close to a quarter of the global fixed-income universe, and is diversified across countries, currencies, and corporate credit issuers.

In addition, EM debt has tended to have a lower correlation to other global asset classes, which may offer investors the potential for diversification.

However, EM debt is often misunderstood, and therefore under-owned and undervalued, in our opinion. Several factors contribute to this: perceived risk vs. actual risk, underrepresentation in global fixed-income indices, inefficient pricing, and historical biases.

We believe this misunderstanding creates potential opportunities for investors who can recognize the asset class’s true strengths and potential for alpha generation.

What are Performance Drivers of EM Debt?

We believe performance of EM debt is primarily driven by two forces: global economic growth and global liquidity conditions.

Improving economic conditions can lead to fundamental improvement in EM countries, which can result in lower risk premiums. And when global interest rates are low and global liquidity is abundant, we have seen EM debt perform well at times.

In market environments with declining inflation or falling interest rates, EM debt has also performed well, as it has tended to display a higher sensitivity to rates. In volatile market environments, EM debt has typically displayed higher volatility than other fixed-income asset classes in the developed market space due to rising risk aversion and investor outflows. But this asset class has historically performed well after periods of high market volatility.

In addition, we believe the U.S. dollar plays an important role in EM debt, and securities may benefit when the U.S. dollar depreciates.

What are Common Myths About EM Debt?

Investing in EM debt is often viewed through a lens of caution and underscored by a common narrative that emphasizes perceived heightened risk. One common myth about the asset class is, “EM debt isn’t right for my investment portfolio,” as some investors often avoid EM debt because of concerns over risk, volatility, and lack of familiarity.

However, we believe this myth, among others, tends to overshadow the nuanced realities of EM debt, and dissecting them reveals an asset class that, when navigated with insight and expertise, presents compelling opportunities for discerning investors.

Original Post
