data.title: UNHappy Ending: How Big Challenges Nuked My UnitedHealth Thesis
data.description:
UnitedHealth Overview

I was wrong.

On December 5, I wrote an article with the super-long title "UnitedHealth: A Dividend Growth Heavyweight Champion Looking To Boost Growth."

Initially, my thesis looked great, as UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) did quite well in recent weeks, after showing a lot of volatility throughout the winter months.

Then, the thesis got knocked out cold, as America's largest health insurance provider lost roughly a fifth of its market cap after reporting first-quarter earnings, making me look bad.

And rightfully so, as I made a huge mistake.

In this article, I'll tell you what I did wrong and everything you need to know about the risk/reward of what used to be one of the best companies for capital gains, anti-cyclical demand (sleep well at night), and consistently rising dividends.

So, let's get to it!

UnitedHealth's Q1 2025 - The Worst Quarter In A Long Time

I have to say, I am somewhat ashamed of being this wrong, as my thesis was nuked by a factor that I actually got right.

To quote Bloomberg:

The company said it was blindsided by rising medical costs as the first quarter closed, upending a forecast it had affirmed just three months ago.

UnitedHealth fell as much as 20% when US markets opened, the most since Sept. 1999. The guidance cut is rare for UnitedHealth, which typically forecasts conservatively and often raises its outlook as the year progresses. - Bloomberg (emphasis added)

Rising medical costs and sticky inflation have been a core part of my strategy for a very long time. That's why I am so bullish on medical equipment providers and companies with pricing power.

I was just under the impression that UnitedHealth had figured out how to stick to guidance despite these headwinds. While it's no excuse, many others seemed to agree with me, as 'nobody' expected this poor EPS result, as the company missed earnings for the first time in a decade. Yes, really.

In other words, this is a case where one of the most reliable performers in the entire stock market suddenly shows weakness. This creates elevated uncertainty, as stocks like UNH are a safe haven for countless investors.

"Unexpected changes" in the company's Optum Health care delivery business that affect reimbursements and elevated care activity in Medicare Advantage health plans are not what anyone wants to see.

According to the company, the jump in quarter-end Medicare expenses was concentrated in physician and outpatient services and even outpaced the higher level of care expenses it had initially prepared for 2025.

The worst part is that a big part of the weakness was tied to Optum Health, as I already briefly mentioned. This segment operates clinics, surgery centers, and home care businesses. As this has been one of the company's fastest-growing profit sources, it's easy to imagine the disappointment among investors and analysts.

I added emphasis to the quote below:

Optum Health has almost 5 million members in insurance-like arrangements, where the company is accountable for the total cost of patients’ care. It treats these patients on behalf of UnitedHealthcare and rival insurers.

The company suggested members in its Optum Health business coming from other insurance plans “may not be appropriately risk-coded,” JPMorgan analyst Lisa Gill wrote in a research note. That would reduce revenue from Medicare. - Bloomberg

This resulted in a $10 billion revenue outlook cut for Optum to a new range of $106 billion to $107 billion.

Here are two more quotes from the company, as it vented some frustration with regard to rising costs:

[...] drug manufacturers continue to increase what they charge Americans, in some cases, 10x what they charge people in Europe.

[...]

Common procedures such as heart bypass surgery, spinal fusions, and heart stents are 4x as expensive in the U.S. as they are in Germany, Australia, and the U.K. Total hip replacements are twice as much. It's simply not sustainable. - UNH Q1 '25 Earnings Call

Moreover, during the earnings call, the company noted that the revised outlook means that about 50% of the full-year earnings will now come from the first half of the year, leaving a potentially weaker performance for the second half of 2025.

This is an issue, especially when considering that UnitedHealth had previously expected a more balanced distribution of earnings.

The good news is that the company affirmed its full-year revenue forecast of $450 billion to $455 billion, which is consistent with the guidance the company released in December.

In general, it's not all bad.

There's Some Good News

The good news is that despite everything we just mentioned, the company's UnitedHealthcare and OptumRx segments are expected to do better than expected, which helps to offset some OptumHealth headwinds.

The $10 billion revenue hit at OptumHealth is mainly attributed to a shift from legacy risk-based arrangements to fee-based arrangements, as I already briefly mentioned, which are neutral to earnings but a headwind for OptumHealth's operating performance.

Essentially, it's an operating problem, as it comes with issues managing the new CMS risk model and other operations. It's not bullish, but something that needs to be said.

In general, the company needs to focus a lot more on costs, as rising costs and the related surge in people who need care (aging population) are presenting long-term challenges for the entire industry.

That's why UnitedHealth is investing in technology, including AI-powered tools to deal with claims. While that may work, it also comes with new challenges, as rejecting care is a very tough thing to do. While it remains to be seen what the judge will rule, it is likely (almost certainly) the reason why one of its executives was assassinated in New York last year.

This incident really showed how many people truly hate the healthcare industry. And I am not using the word "hate" lightly here.

Cutting costs in an environment of rising demand is one of the toughest things to do. I do not envy these decision-makers, even if I assume some of them make way more money than me.

With that said, overall demand remains strong, as UnitedHealthcare expects to add up to 800,000 new members in its Medicare Advantage programs, which include individuals and group plans. It also benefits from new state expansions in Kentucky, New York, and the Sunshine State, Florida.

As a result, UnitedHealth is sticking to its long-term target, which sees between 13% and 16% annual earnings growth.

[...] we're committed to improving our performance in the rest of 2025 and into '26, and in doing so to delivering consistent positive results for you and returning to our long-term earnings per share growth target of 13% to 16%. - UNH Q1 '25 Earnings Call

Now, let's look into what this could mean for investors.

The Risk/Reward In UNH Stock

For what it's worth, the best thing about steep sell-offs is that a lot will be priced in.

While I am writing this, UNH yields 1.9%. This dividend has a 30% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 14%.

It also trades at just 13.5x 2026E EPS of $33.55, which is a mile below its multi-decade average of 17.6x (FactSet data).

Moreover, it's interesting that analysts already had lowered their expectations, forecasting just 8% EPS growth this year. 2026 and 2027 are expected to see 13-14% growth, in line with the company's longer-term guidance.

While I believe 2025 and 2026 will likely both be below 10%, I still like UNH at these levels, as the risk/reward is good.

However, because the company has made it very clear that both inflation and secular growth in costs are issues, UNH is not a high-conviction idea.

Although I think UNH has the ability to deliver substantial long-term total returns, I prefer other places to put money, especially because I believe in 'higher for longer' inflation and interest rates.

In light of these challenges, I will likely write a special article next week on inflation and secular challenges facing some of the market's most beloved stocks.

Takeaway

I made a mistake with UnitedHealth.

While the company still holds long-term potential, its recent earnings miss, especially from the Optum Health segment, has raised red flags. A lot of them.

Rising costs, especially in healthcare, remain a major challenge, and although the company is adjusting its approach, I think it's fair to say a ton of uncertainty remains.

Fortunately, for new investors, the stock is currently trading below its historical valuation, which makes it an attractive entry point for everyone looking for insurance exposure.

However, it's no longer a high-conviction pick for me.

With inflationary pressures lingering and despite a bigger focus on efficiencies, I'm cautious about its near-term outlook and prefer other opportunities that align more closely with my current strategy.

data.title: State Street Q1: Earnings Beat With Positive Outlook
data.description:
I rate State Street Corporation (NYSE:STT) a Buy, for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. The company just declared Q1 earnings, beating the estimates, showing growth in all key metrics. Growth in assets under custody shows their business model is working by retaining clients and growing their assets. The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Q1 2025 earnings were strong, showing increases in revenue and income.
  • Quarterly net income of $644 million was up +39%.
  • Assets under custody grew 6% and Assets under management grew 9% for the quarter.
  • Pays a rising quarterly dividend with an annual yield of 3.82%.
  • Business model focused on institutional clients, which tends to last many years.
  • Opportunity to grow internationally.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Sell 2.42 (as of 4/17/2025).

Global Provider of Financial Services to Institutions

Headquartered in Boston, State Street is

“one of the world's leading providers of financial services to institutional investors including investment servicing, investment management and investment research and trading. With $46.6 trillion in assets under custody and/or administration and $4.7 trillion* in assets under management as of December 31, 2024, State Street operates globally in more than 100 geographic markets and employs approximately 53,000 worldwide.”

The company offers a broad range of financial products and services through various subsidiaries, including State Street Bank and Trust company. Their core business is investment servicing and investment management, with a large presence in ETFs.

Within State Street Global Advisors, the $4.7 trillion in AUM breaks out as shown in the chart below. The largest segment is ETFs, which are $1.6 trillion, up +24%, part of an $11 trillion market.

Q1 2025 Earnings Beat

STT reported a strong Q1 2025, kicking off the year with multiple beats vs estimates. At a high level, revenues rose 5%, expenses dropped 3%; therefore net income increased substantially +39%. Also, the key metrics surrounding client accounts (AUC/AUM) grew, which is a good sign for the company’s future.

On April 17, 2025, the company declared Q1 2025 earnings, with the following quarterly financial highlights:

  • Total revenue of $3.28 billion grew +5%, fee revenue up +6%
  • Expenses of $2.4 billion were down 3%
  • Net income of $644 million was up +39%
  • Earnings per share (EPS) of $2.04 increased +49%
  • Investment Servicing AUC/A increased 6% to $46.7 billion
  • Investment Management AUM increased 9% to $4.7 billion.

Slower Growing Performance vs. Other Financial Services

A $10,000 investment in STT held over 10 years would be worth $13,780 today. This performance falls short of the financial services sector as represented by SPDR Fund (XLF), which achieved almost double the growth. Given their slower growth asset servicing business, the upside is once they have the business it is hard to lose it; however, the business doesn't have the faster growing businesses such as trading, investment banking, or charging higher fees on retail investor accounts.

Growing Valuation

STT has a long history of increasing dividends, as shown visually in the chart below. This trend is encouraging to investors because it serves as a base for the stock. In times of volatility, the dividend becomes more valuable as the stock price goes down, making the stock more attractive as the dividend yield rises.

Over the past 10 years and more, STT has a history of growing the dividend, making it a good contender for buying and holding for the long term. For example, one share of STT earned a dividend of $1.16 in 2014. That same share now collects $3.04 in 2025.

In comparison to other financial services companies, STT’s metrics are in line with the industry. In fact, their price earnings ratio is the lowest in the group, indicating STT may be trading at a discount.

Morgan Stanley (MS) is the largest of the comparables, as ranked by market capitalization.

Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), a fairly new company, has the second-highest dividend yield of 4.2%, the highest dividend growth rate over the past 3 years, and at $26 billion in market cap is the most similar in size to STT.

T. Rowe Price Group, Inc. (TROW) has the highest dividend yield of 6.0%, but the worst 3 year total return, declining -28.6%.

The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) is the second-largest by market capitalization, with the second highest 3 year total return.

The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK)has the lowest dividend yield of 2.5% but the highest 3 year total return of 79.7%.

Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM) is the newest of the group, going public on December 1, 2022. BAM trades with the highest price earnings ratio of 25.8.

Outlook

Short term, companies such as STT face uncertainty driven by government policies around trade, tasks to reduce the deficit, tax policy, and deregulation. Their clients have the same issues, which will determine which services they will need. If STT can address the issues effectively with clients, this time of stress will build stronger relationships.

Given the uncertainty in capital markets, it can be expected that fee revenue may be compromised with lower market valuations; hence AUM will go down.

STT will find future growth opportunities doing business internationally.

Risks To Rating

The main risks to STT are in government policy regarding trade, taxes, and regulation as these affect asset valuation and fee revenue. Institutional clients may react to changes in policy or position themselves in such a way to exploit opportunities or avoid threats.

A secondary risk is competition. The financial services sector is highly competitive, therefore changes in strategy from a competitor could pose a significant threat to STT. Given the lower margin business of asset servicing, combined with the core competency of their scale, puts a wide moat around the company from competition.

Bottom Line

I rate STT a "Buy" for capital appreciation focused investors looking for long-term buy and hold investments. As the majority of STT’s business is asset servicing, the business model builds in a high level of continuity, making it easier for a client to continue using STT vs. changing. The company pays a quarterly dividend, currently yielding 3.82%. STT has a history of growing the dividend consistently over time, making the investment more attractive with this feature.

Q1 2025 earnings released on April 17th were very positive, showing growth in revenues and net income, driving a beat in earnings. Further growth in assets under custody to $46.7 trillion shows the company’s strategy is working, drawing in more clients who remain at the firm.

Risks to the company are driven by government policy where trade, taxes, and regulation are currently being debated. The outlook for STT is still positive, as the institutions will adjust as needed, most likely staying with the service provider selected.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Return Of Dividends: Why DHS Is Having A Moment
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

At WisdomTree, our journey began in 2006 with a foundational belief: that dividends matter. The market regime following the global financial crisis1 often rewarded high-growth, non-dividend-paying companies—particularly in the U.S. Technology, Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services sectors. A core tenet of portfolio construction became easy to ignore: the importance of defense.

For much of the past two decades, dividend strategies were overshadowed by the rise of mega-cap growth. But there's a principle in investing—defense can appear as annoying underperformance…until you need it. In 2025, with rising volatility and a difficult-to-predict Trump Administration, there is a growing market rotation away from the Magnificent 7,2 and dividend-paying companies have come back in focus.

Figure 1: Standardized Performance

So far, 2025 has been a rough ride—and in a year where everything feels a bit painful return-wise, the WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Fund (DHS) has been less painful. Down −3.5% year-to-date (YTD), it's held up meaningfully better than the Russell 1000 Value Index (−8.1%) and especially the S&P 500 (−13.6%). The Nasdaq 100 Index has been the leader for some time, emphasizing exposure to the mega-cap technology-oriented companies we mentioned earlier, and it is down 16.9% over this YTD period. When the market starts to question the price it pays for forward-looking expectations, cash in hand—in the form of dividends—suddenly looks like a more favorable deal.

Zoom out to the one-year view, and DHS has maintained its prominence. While broad value and the S&P 500 still reflect losses (−2.3% and −1.4%, respectively), DHS is up 8.6%. Again, the Nasdaq 100 Index faced the toughest result, down 3.0%. That's not a fluke. It's a signal. Market leadership is starting to rotate, and strategies that favor durability over disruption are gaining ground.

But move further out—three, five and 10 years—and the gravitational pull of megacap tech becomes unavoidable. The S&P 500, supercharged by a handful of dominant growth stocks, lapped both dividend and value benchmarks, and the Nasdaq 100 Index did even better. It's a reminder that in certain regimes, concentration can look like genius…until it doesn't.

In our opinion, the point isn't that dividends always win. It's that they show up when you need them most—and they do so without requiring you to bet the farm on what's next in AI, semiconductors or space colonization.

Figure 2: DHS's Near-Term Outperformance as Defense Became More Important

Sometimes the standard periods of multiple years can mask the experience that many of us might remember during individual years. For example:

  • The S&P 500 Index had a negative calendar-year return during two of the last 10 full calendar years, 2018 and 2022. Many may remember 2022 in particular, as the Federal Reserve was hiking its policy rate quickly to combat inflation. U.S. equities and fixed income were challenged. While people may remember the −18.11% return of the S&P 500 Index, they may not remember that the Nasdaq 100 Index was down more than 32%.
  • DHS delivered a strongly defensive result in 2022—a positive 7.88% return while the other benchmarks in figure 3 were all negative. However, 2018 was not as successful.
  • We believe that many people have not been looking at DHS because of what is clear in 2023 and 2024, when the Fund dramatically trailed both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 indexes. This is that "annoying underperformance" that we mentioned earlier.

Figure 3: The Last 10 Calendar Years

What You Don't Own Matters Just as Much as What You Do

One look at figure 4 and the story becomes obvious: DHS is not trying to be everything to everyone. It's deliberately not chasing tech. While the S&P 500 leans hard into Information Technology (north of 30% exposure), DHS sits comfortably with single-digit weight. That's not an accident—it's the product of a strategy that asks a different question: "Who's paying shareholders today?" rather than "Who's growing the fastest?"

That sector tilt—away from the frothy and toward the fundamental—defines the defensive DNA of DHS. It's right there in the over-weight allocations to Consumer Staples, Utilities, Energy and Health Care. These aren't the sectors that dominate headlines, but they do tend to hold up when the headlines turn sour.

Financials and Energy together account for over 30% of DHS—two sectors that, while cyclical, also represent valuation discipline and free cash flow. These are businesses that have had to prove themselves in high-rate environments. And in a world where capital is no longer free, that starts to matter again.

The under-weight to Information Technology, and to a lesser extent to Communication Services, has felt like a liability in the growth-dominated years. But in 2025, it's looking more like a cushion.

Figure 4: DHS Emphasized a Defensive Sector Orientation

When You Don't Own the Magnificent 7

Look at DHS's top holdings. There's no Apple. No Nvidia (NVDA). No Microsoft (MSFT). No Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META) or Tesla (TSLA). In fact, the total exposure to the Magnificent 7 is zero. This is a portfolio that has deliberately not owned the market's most talked-about—and most concentrated—drivers of return over the past five years.

Names like Philip Morris (PM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Exxon Mobil (XOM) and AbbVie (ABBV) - these are household names, yes, but also steady compounders with strong balance sheets and consistent dividend policies. In fact, the top 10 names in DHS account for over 42% of the portfolio, and every one of them pays you to own them.

Contrast that with the S&P 500, where more than a third of the index is concentrated in just 10 companies—seven of which are tech-driven giants. That's a bet on continued dominance by a handful of firms that have already defied gravity for a decade.

The Russell 1000 Value Index may be Value, but its top holdings do overlap with those of the S&P 500 Index and total only about 18% of the portfolio.

DHS expresses something different: intentional concentration in income-generating companies. It's not designed to beat the S&P 500 Index in momentum-driven bull markets. It's designed to act like ballast when the future starts to feel uncertain.

Figure 5: DHS Avoided the Magnificent 7

The Payoff: Real Income, Right Now

DHS offers a 3.88% dividend yield3—nearly double the Russell 1000 Value Index and more than triple the S&P 500. That's not a rounding error. That's a fundamentally different approach to equity investing.

Where most portfolios today are implicitly betting on multiple expansion, DHS delivers a return stream that doesn't require perfect timing or endless growth. It pays you to be patient. It pays you through the volatility.

Importantly, this isn't yield for yield's sake. These are not distressed businesses with unsustainable payout ratios. DHS is built from the ground up and seeks to mitigate the risk of exposure to value traps and companies paying unsustainable dividends. The highest weight is not in companies with the highest yields but rather in those companies that are distributing the most cash in aggregate to their shareholders.

In a world where cash once again has a cost and equity markets are repricing risk, income is strategy—not just an outcome.

Figure 6: DHS Tilted toward Higher Dividend-Yielding Companies

Valuation Discipline in an Expensive Market

With the S&P 500 trading at 22 times forward earnings, investors are once again leaning heavily on the idea that growth will justify the price. The Nasdaq 100 Index saw an even higher valuation. The Russell 1000 Value Index is more modest, but still sits at 17.5 times. Neither feels particularly cheap by historical standards, especially with real rates now positive and capital no longer free.

Then there's DHS—clocking in at just 14.0 times.

That number isn't just lower; it's anchored in a portfolio of dividend payers with high return-on-equity metrics and a strict valuation discipline at the time of rebalancing. It reflects a different logic: don't pay up for potential, pay less for profitability that already exists.

In an era where multiple expansion may be limited, we believe starting valuation matters more than ever.

Figure 7: DHS Dialed Down the Estimated P/E Ratio

Conclusion: When the Market Narrative Changes, So Should the Toolkit

For much of the last decade, being under-weight in tech or over-weight in dividends felt like a drag—an anchor in a market that rewarded speed over stability, potential over profit. But in 2025, the narrative is shifting, and DHS is meeting the moment.

In a market defined by extreme concentration, extended valuations and rising macroeconomic uncertainty, DHS offers something refreshingly scarce: balance. It is a portfolio built not on dreams of disruption but on durable cash flows, capital return and valuation discipline. The Fund has delivered strong relative performance in a tough environment, and its sector and stock positioning shows a clear intentionality: real businesses, paying real income, at reasonable prices.

Where others are asking, "How much longer can the Magnificent 7 lead?", DHS has already moved on.

1 The global financial crisis played out in global markets during portions of 2008 and 2009. After this period, interest rates across much of the developed world were at or near record lows for extended periods, an environment that was very favorable for growth-oriented equities.

2 Refers to Apple, Amazon.com, Alphabet, Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Nvidia and Tesla.

3 As of 4/15/25, DHS's SEC 30-day Yield = 3.94%.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree's London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU's Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor's degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.
